explicit_text: My dad was an actor and appeared in many of the Sessue Hayakawa pictures. Sessue Hayakawa was a well-known Japanese actor in Hollywood during the silent film era. I even appeared as a boy in one of his films, which was shot on location at Big Bear Lake. When Sessue Hayakawa's pictures played in theaters, we kids would perform on stage before the show started.

explicit_text: I recall the 1960s and the era of John F. Kennedy. During that time, we had bumper stickers on our car that said 'Make Love Not War,' reflecting the sentiments around the Vietnam War. JFK's presidency was a significant backdrop to those years, influencing the culture and politics we experienced. It was a time of change and activism.

explicit_text: I had a Barracuda car in the 1960s, and my little brother Dave also had one. The Barracuda was quite old and had many issues like a broken gas tank and speedometer, but he loved it dearly. I remember going to the airport in a Barracuda with Andy, who was a fraternity brother. My dad used to drive a Barracuda too, and it was quite popular among his fraternity friends.

explicit_text: I remember driving up in Fumi's little Datsun with a Siamese cat that was very noisy during the 400-mile trip. The Datsun was part of our family life at that time.

explicit_text: In the late 1910s, I went to Tulsa after my uncle lost his money and moved there. I attended Tulsa Business College but struggled financially. I worked in various jobs including at my uncle's store and later got a job with the wealthy Travis family. Tulsa was an important place in my early adult life as I tried to find work and continue my education.

explicit_text: While in Oklahoma, I was told by my uncle to go to Sand Springs Park where they had summer games and shops. Sand Springs was part of my early efforts to earn a living before moving on to other opportunities.

The Travis family and their corporation played a key role in my early work life in Tulsa.

explicit_text: After realizing I couldn't afford dental college in Tulsa, I followed a friend to Chicago because it had many dental schools. I took a job as a butler in a wealthy family, which helped me earn money while attending art school. Life in Chicago was challenging with tight finances, but I managed to go to the Art Institute and work simultaneously. Chicago was a turning point in my pursuit of education and work.

explicit_text: I attended the Art Institute of Chicago, located on Michigan Boulevard, while working as a butler to support myself. The school cost $20 a month, and I earned $7 a week, which made it difficult financially. I walked about 17 blocks to get to the school and enjoyed visiting the museum there. Although I tried to pursue art, I was advised that being an artist was a poor business, which influenced my decisions later.
